title: Big O Notation
description: The formal language for the growth shapes from Time and Space Complexity: dropping constants, the common complexity classes, and why worst case matters.
objectives: Understand Big O as a formal notation for growth shape
Know why constants and lower-order terms are dropped
Recognize the common complexity classes in order
Understand Big O's focus on worst-case behavior

sections.text: 1. Recap: We've Been Using This Informally
Time Complexity already used O(1), O(n), and O(n²) without formally defining the notation itself. Let's make it precise.
Big O notation describes the upper-bound growth rate of an algorithm's time or space usage as input size (n) grows, focusing on the dominant factor and ignoring constants.

Big O cares about the growth shape, not the exact count. 2n still grows linearly — doubling the input still roughly doubles the work, the same shape as n alone. The constant factor (2, in this case) is dropped, and we simply write O(n).

sections.code:
// Suppose an algorithm does n² + n operations total
// As n grows large, n² completely dominates n — we call this O(n²), not O(n² + n)

sections.text: For large n, n² grows so much faster than n that the +n barely matters. Big O keeps only the dominant (fastest-growing) term and drops the rest — this is what makes it a clean description of long-term growth shape, not an exact operation count.
5. Common Complexity Classes, Fastest to Slowest

rows: Binary Search — a preview, its own upcoming topic
A single loop over the input
sort() — recall Sorting using sort()'s complexity
Nested loops over the same input
Certain brute-force recursive approaches, covered later

sections.text: This is only a preview — Binary Search, coming up soon, is the classic example of O(log n): an algorithm that cuts the remaining problem roughly in half with each step, rather than checking every element one by one. log n grows extremely slowly — doubling n only adds one more step, a dramatic improvement over linear time for large inputs.

Big O, as typically used in interviews and DSA, most commonly describes the worst case — the maximum work an algorithm could possibly need, for the least favorable input. This gives a reliable guarantee: 'this algorithm will never do worse than this,' rather than an average that could hide a bad edge case.

sections.title: Describing a trip as "a few hours" instead of "3 hours 47 minutes and 12 seconds"
sections.text: Saying a trip takes "a few hours" captures the useful shape of the information — long enough to plan around — without getting lost in exact-to-the-second precision that would vary by traffic, weather, and every other minor factor. Big O is that same kind of useful approximation: it captures the growth shape that actually matters for decision-making, deliberately ignoring precise constants that vary by machine and implementation.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Writing O(2n) or O(n² + n) instead of simplifying to O(n) or O(n²) — Big O always drops constants and lower-order terms.
Assuming Big O gives an exact operation count — it describes growth shape, not a precise number.
Confusing best case, average case, and worst case — Big O in interviews and DSA almost always refers to worst case unless stated otherwise.
Assuming a 'better' Big O class is always faster in absolute terms for every input size — the comparison is about growth as n gets large, not necessarily at small, fixed sizes.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What does Big O notation describe?
questions.answer: The upper-bound growth rate of an algorithm's time or space usage as input size grows, focusing on the dominant factor.

questions.question: Why are constants dropped in Big O, e.g. writing O(n) instead of O(2n)?
questions.answer: Because Big O describes growth shape, not an exact count — 2n and n both grow linearly, the same fundamental shape as input size increases.

questions.question: List the common complexity classes from fastest to slowest.
questions.answer: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2ⁿ).

questions.question: Does Big O typically describe best case, average case, or worst case?
questions.answer: Worst case, most commonly — the maximum possible work for the least favorable input, giving a reliable upper-bound guarantee.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Big O notation formally describes an algorithm's growth shape as input size grows, dropping constants (O(2n) simplifies to O(n)) and lower-order terms (O(n² + n) simplifies to O(n²)), since those don't affect the fundamental growth pattern for large inputs. The common classes, from fastest to slowest, are O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), and O(2ⁿ) — with O(log n), previewed via Binary Search, and O(n log n), already seen in sort()'s complexity, being especially important benchmarks. Big O, as typically used in interviews, describes worst-case behavior: a guarantee about the maximum work required, regardless of how favorable the input happens to be.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Big O Notation
sections.text: Big O = the growth SHAPE, not an exact count. Drop constants, drop lower-order terms. O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ). Usually worst case.
